coredata.dc:description: Microwave irradiation is used in this study to synthesize poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate)/chitosan (P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan) semi-IPN hydrogels, which are synthesized using one-pot polymerization techniques with ammonium persulfate (APS) as an initiator. The first step was to synthesize a (P(AM-co-HEMA) network in a chitosan aqueous solution, which formed a semi-IPN structure. Nanocomposite membranes of P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan membranes with varying Zinc oxide concentrations i.e. 2.5, 5, and 10 wt. % gravimetrically were tested for gas permeability measurements of pure oxygen gas at feed pressures ranging from 1 to 20 kg/cm2. The permeability of the membranes to oxygen ranged from 0.0963 to 0.2634 Barrer for the pristine Poly (Acrylamide-co-2-Hydroxyethyl Methacrylate)/Chitosan Semi-IPN membrane, and 5 and 10 wt. % ZnO loaded blend membranes showed good resistance and zero permeability at feed pressures of 1–5 kg/cm2 and 0.0341 to 0.0364 Barrer for 10 wt. % ZnO loaded blend membranes by increasing the feed pressures at the feed chamber. The nanocomposite membranes were tested for antimicrobial properties and found to be a more effective antimicrobial material. The results of the soil burial tests show that the prepared membranes are biodegradable. Mechanical strength is exceptional under normal conditions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microwave irradiation is used in this study to synthesize poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate)/chitosan (P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan) semi-IPN hydrogels, which are synthesized using one-pot polymerization techniques with ammonium persulfate (APS) as an initiator. The first step was to synthesize a (P(AM-co-HEMA) network in a chitosan aqueous solution, which formed a semi-IPN structure. Nanocomposite membranes of P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan membranes with varying Zinc oxide concentrations i.e. 2.5, 5, and 10 wt. % gravimetrically were tested for gas permeability measurements of pure oxygen gas at feed pressures ranging from 1 to 20 kg/cm2. The permeability of the membranes to oxygen ranged from 0.0963 to 0.2634 Barrer for the pristine Poly (Acrylamide-co-2-Hydroxyethyl Methacrylate)/Chitosan Semi-IPN membrane, and 5 and 10 wt. % ZnO loaded blend membranes showed good resistance and zero permeability at feed pressures of 1–5 kg/cm2 and 0.0341 to 0.0364 Barrer for 10 wt. % ZnO loaded blend membranes by increasing the feed pressures at the feed chamber. The nanocomposite membranes were tested for antimicrobial properties and found to be a more effective antimicrobial material. The results of the soil burial tests show that the prepared membranes are biodegradable. Mechanical strength is exceptional under normal conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: There has been a rapid increase in the production of plastic substances used for commodity and food packaging over the last two decades. Poly-olefin is the best candidate, but it is accompanied by a greater amount of plastic waste. This will occur as a result of waste accumulation in nature due to high-quality chemical, mechanical, weathering, and non-biodegradability [1,2]. Over the last two decades, there has been a rapid increase in the production of plastic substances used for commodity and food packaging purposes. Poly-olefin is the best candidate, but it is accompanied by a greater amount of plastic waste. This will happen as a result of waste accumulation in nature [3,4].

Plastic products are generally used since their commercialization due to their abundance of availability, cost-effectiveness, and convenience, as a result, dumping of huge quantities of plastic as waste [5,6]. However, for the use and throw packaging material, the prevalence of food-borne infection risk remains high, necessitating approaches to hinder microorganism reproduction and growth [7]. Developing biodegradable packaging material and incorporating antimicrobial agents on it will be the possible solution to pollution by plastic packaging materials and food remains fresh for a longer time, and reducing the food born diseases [8]. New generation antimicrobial, biodegradable polymeric films have found a wide variety of applications in which food packaging is the prioritized one [9,10], in the textile industry [11], and medicine [12]. The advantages of using cellulose derivative polymers as packaging material include increased airtightness, tensile strength, and optical transparency [13–15]. The disadvantage of cellulose membranes is their lack of antimicrobial properties [16]. Chitosan is another natural polymer that is extracted from chitin through partial or complete deacetylation. Chitosan has been shown to be antimicrobial in nature [17,18], excellent film-forming property, easily degradability, anticoagulant, and biocompatibility [19–21]. Because of its unique properties, chitosan is regarded as an emerging and promising dressing material in the biomedical field. Despite its nature, chitosan membranes have the disadvantages of poor tensile strength, opaqueness, and brittleness, resulting in limited applications as an independent chotosan [22]. To make commercially durable by combining other material in to chitosan like cellulose will compensate for the deficiencies of both components [23]. Hydrogels are three-dimensional copolymeric materials held together by covalent bonding and van-der walls force. Polymer-based hydrogel was a promising candidate for several biomedical and pharmaceutical release devices. Nanoparticles embedded in semi-interpenetrating (semi-IPNs) polymeric hydrogels with improved mechanical toughness and biocompatibility could find widespread use in biomedicine. Because of the large fluctuations in physiological pH, hydrogels have been widely used [24,25]. Semi-IPN, or semi-interpenetrating polymer network, is a biphasic polymer system in which one phase is cross-linked when the other phase is present [26]. According to Sperling et al. [27], there is no chemical bonding between the component polymers in semi-IPN, but one polymer phase exists in the cross-linked network. Hydrogels consist of three-dimensional copolymeric materials that are linked to each other held by covalent bonding and weak van-der walls force or H-bonding. A promising candidate for several biomedical and pharmaceutical release devices was polymer-based hydrogel. Nanoparticles embedded in semi-interpenetrating (semi-IPNs) polymeric hydrogels with enhanced mechanical toughness and biocompatibility may find widespread biomedical application. Hydrogels have been widely used due to large fluctuations in physiological pH [24,25]. Semi-IPN, or semi-interpenetrating polymer network, is a biphasic polymer system in which one phase is cross-linked in the presence of the other phase [26]. There is no chemical bonding between the component polymers in semi-IPN, but one polymer phase exists in the cross-linked network, according to Sperling et al. [27].

The occurrence of a wide variety of synthetic and natural polymers will lead to thinking about different options and tunable properties. Indeed, they will open up great potential as packaging materials and therapeutic systems. Furthermore, cross-linked hydrogel networks will act as shield antimicrobial and medicines from possible unsafe environments such as microorganisms, enzymes, and pH (low, basic), [28]. Numbers of natural polymers are used as starting materials to prepare hydrophilic copolymers from the plant (cellulose, alginate) and animal sources (chitin, chitosan). The applications of such materials include orthopedics, drug delivery, ophthalmology, and medical devices [29,30]. In the literature, it was reported that the synthesis, characterization, and measurement of acrylic compounds as packaging materials, biomaterials, and drug delivery devices [31,32]. Combining natural polymers like chitosan and synthetic polymer or hydrogel copolymers may emerge as in better candidate for biodegradable, antimicrobial food packaging material [31].

Several strategies for overcoming these flaws in composite membranes have been investigated [29–32]. Antimicrobial properties of composite membranes, for example, have been improved through modification or combination with antimicrobial agents [33]. It was discovered that adding inhibitors like TiO2, SiO2, MgO, and KMnO4 to biodegradable materials improved their physicochemical properties by Yorghanlu et al. [33], Jabraili et al. [34], Pirsa and Chavoshizadeh [35] as well as antimicrobial properties by Hosseini et al. [36], Mohammadi et al. [37] and Pirsa et al. [38] and Ning Y et al. [39] thermally stable, and water-soluble. Phenylacetic acid (PA) is a naturally antibacterial material that is acidic, PA inhibits a wide range of Gram-positive bacteria, Gram-negative bacteria, and fungi, and its primary site of action is thought to be in the cell wall. Similar to the mechanism of action of lysozyme [40]. Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles (NP's) have long been used as an inorganic filler, which acts as a bacterial inhibitor due to their long duration, broad-spectrum antibacterial properties, and mechanical and thermal stability [41–44]. The widely accepted concept is that the three mechanisms of inhibition, as reported in the literature [45–47] are: (i) metal ion solubilization; (ii) contact adsorption; and (iii) photocatalytic production of reactive oxygen moiety. In continuation, ZnO NP;s have a larger specific surface area, which elevates contact with microbial organisms like bacteria and facilitates additional active sites for the photocatalytic process [48–50], resulting in an increased antibacterial effect. The use of hydrogel/chitosan hybrid copolymeric membranes as base materials can provide the next generation synergistic microbial inhibition. As per the reported research work, the incorporation of ZnO with an oxidizing agent may improve the antibacterial activity of composite/nanocomposite membranes [51]. In food packaging materials, the addition of a small amount of ZnO NP's to the chitosan and hydrogels like poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) or poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate)/chitosan composite membranes shows performance in improved residual pesticide degradation rate, significantly reduced decay rate of fruits and vegetables and provides excellent mechanical properties as compared to the pristine chitosan membranes [52–57]. As a packaging material to be used for foods, this nanocomposite film may contact moisture from foods or the outside environment. Although the introduction of the hydrophilic component might be helpful for degradation, it could also affect the swelling performance, oxygen permeability, transparency, and mechanical strength of the film.

In the present investigation, we mainly focused on the combination of synthetic hydrogel and natural antimicrobial polymer, and in addition to this combining these with nanoparticles to develop next-generation antimicrobial, biodegradable food packaging material. In an attempt to meet our goal poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) hydrogels were synthesized via microwave in presence of chitosan as the main body of the biodegradable copolymeric membranes and the ZnO nanoparticles were incorporated into the base matrix to synergistically enhance its antimicrobial properties. The combination of poly (acrylamide-co-2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate) with chitosan and ZnO produced a nanocomposite copolymeric membrane, which was studied for its potential application as a biodegradable antimicrobial packaging material.

2-Hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA, 97%), acrylamide monomer (AM, 98.5%), and ammonium persulfate (APS, 99%), were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Zinc chloride, Polyvinyl alcohol (MWT 1,25,000 g/mol), Glutaraldehyde, and NaOH are all purchased from s.d. Fine Chemicals in India. Throughout the experiment, double-distilled water was used. Without further purification, all of the reagents were used.

Preparation According to the literature, ZnO nanoparticles were synthesized by direct precipitation using zinc chloride and NaOH as precursors [58]. In this study, deionized water was used to make an aqueous solution (0.2 M) of zinc chloride (ZnCl2.6H2O) and a solution (0.4 M) of NaOH. At room temperature, the NaOH solution was slowly added to the zinc chloride solution while vigorously stirring, resulting in the formation of a white suspension. The obtained white product was centrifuged for 30 min at 3000 rpm before being washed three times with distilled water and then with absolute alcohol. The obtained product was calcined at 500 °C in an air atmosphere for 3 h to obtain fine ZnO powder.

The P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymer was prepared by free radical polymerization technique in chitosan solution at an aqueous medium. In free radical polymerization, it also takes part in the free radical polymerization reaction. As a result, more branched side chains are organized in the copolymeric network formed during free radical polymerization [59,60]. The composition of the chemicals used for the reaction is tabulated in Table 1 .

In a 2% acetic acid aqueous solution, the AAm monomer, HEMA monomer, and chitosan polymer mixture were dissolved and 0.2 wt. % of ammonium persulfate (APS) was added as a free radical initiator. The resulting mixture was flushed with nitrogen for 1 h to remove dissolved oxygen and other gaseous impurities that could act as inhibitors during the reaction. After obtaining a clear homogeneous mixture by stirring, the flask was microwave irradiated for 3 min at 640 W in a domestic microwave oven. For synthesis, an IFB 30 L convection microwave oven (Model no 30BRC2; 2200 Watts, operation frequency of 2450 MHz frequency) and a power output range of 0–900W with continuous adjustment were used. The resulting semi-IPN copolymer was obtained after the reaction was completed. The reaction mixture was allowed to cool before being precipitated with a 4:1 methanol: water mixture. The obtained raw material was filtered by a vacuum pump, several times washed with water followed by drying in an incubator at 70 °C temperature to attain constant weight [61]. Fig. 1 depicts a schematic representation of the formation of (P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN hydrogels.

5 g of P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymer was dissolved in 80 mL of 2% acetic acid aqueous solution by constant stirring until the solution thickened to a viscous slurry. The ZnO nanoparticles were sonicated in 10 mL of water for 30 min with an ultrasonicator (Bio Technics, Model: BTI-48, 100 W) in a water medium before being incorporated into the previously synthesized P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan solution [62]. The gravimetric ratio of the synthesized ZnO nanoparticles is 2.5, 5, and 10 wt. % w.r.t. the synthesized copolymer. To achieve a homogeneous solution, the entire mixture was constantly stirred for 4 h. To obtain uniform-thickness membranes, the prepared viscous solution was cast as a membrane on a clean and dry glass plate using a doctor's blade (Elicometer, model 3700). Before peeling the caste membranes off the glass plate, they were allowed to dry at room temperature. The detailed steps of P(AM-co-HEMA)/Chitosan synthesis and membrane fabrication were shown in a flow chart in Fig. 2 . The fabricated nanocomposite membranes were labeled as M@ZnO-0, M@ZnO-2.5. M@ZnO-5 and M@ZnO-10 are based on their ZnO content of 2.5, 5, and 10%, respectively. Formulations and thicknesses of P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN nanocomposite copolymer Membranes are tabulated in Table 2 .

FTIR (Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy) spectra of P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan was measured using a Schemadzu (Chiyoda-Ku, Tokyo, Japan, Model: IR-Affinity-I) FTIR spectrometer in the 4000 to 400 cm−1 range. Copolymeric membrane samples were ground thoroughly with spectroscopic grade KBr, and pellets were formed by pressing at 400–450 kg/cm2 hydraulic pressure.

The transparency of the synthesized nanocomposite copolymeric film was measured using a UV–visible spectrophotometer and the ASTM: D1746 standard (Shimadzu, Model UV-1900i). The prepared films were cut into small rectangular shapes measuring 3 cm × 5 cm, and each rectangular film was carefully placed in front of the source beam. The optical transparencies of the samples were measured in the 570 nm range using the previously described procedure [63,64]. The uniform thickness samples were taken into account to ensure that the aforementioned result was minimized and that there was no significant variation among the samples.

Mechanical properties such as tensile strength (MPa) and elongation at break (percent) were measured for all prepared films using the universal testing machine (UTM) (Hounsfield, UK), model H 25 KS, with an operating head load of 5 kN, according to ASTM D 412. Membrane strips were placed between the testing machine's grips. The grip length was set at 5 cm, and the testing speed was set at 5 mm/min. The following equation was used to calculate tensile strength:

After gold sputter coating on the specimen surface, the surface morphology of M− M@ZnO-0, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 were assessed for the Scanning electron microscope; model Jeol, Model 7900F. SEM images were taken after sputter coating of gold on the surface of the specimen.

The prepared films were gently washed with ethanol to disinfect them and remove any dust that had become entrapped on the surface. The films were kept sterile to allow the alcohol to evaporate from the surface. The M@ZnO-0, M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 membranes were tested for antimicrobial activity using the agar diffusion protocol, as previously reported [65]. The Agar diffusion protocol is also known as the Kirby–Bauer disc diffusion procedure. The procedure entails swabbing the test agar plate with a standardized concentration of the test organism (bacteria) and then placing plain and modified membrane discs containing a different loading of ZnO NP's concentration on the bacteria lawn. The inhibition zone was used to test the antimicrobial property of ZnO NPs against Escherichia coli (gram-negative bacteria) and Staphylococcus aureus (gram-positive bacteria). The focus on E. Coli and S. aureus is because these bacteria are found in food, the environment, and the intestines of living beings, including humans. E. coli bacteria are a diverse group. While the majority of E. coli are harmless, others can cause illness. Some E. coli strains can cause diarrhea, while others can cause urinary tract infections, respiratory illness, pneumonia, and other illnesses. S. Aureus is a gram-positive bacteria that cause a wide range of clinical conditions. Infections caused by this pathogen are common in both community and hospital settings.

A soil burial test was performed on a laboratory scale to evaluate the biodegradability of the P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN nanocomposite copolymeric membranes (M@ZnO-0) and M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 membranes. The synthesized Copolymers and nanocopolymeric films were made into dog bone-shaped specimens exactly 10 cm by length. The soil burial experiment was carried out in two sets one is for 10 days and the second set is for 20 days. Alluvial soil in wet conditions was added in plastic containers with perforated pores at its wall and bottom for better air and water circulation. In the testing stage, the soil was maintained moist by water and stored outdoor. The molded dog bone-shaped films were buried in the soil at a depth of 10 cm from the surface and subjected to the action of soil microorganisms [66,67]. After the test, the samples were carefully removed, gently washed with distilled water, and finally dried in an incubator at 70 °C temperature before being stored in desiccators.

A gas permeameter was used to measure the permeability of single gas such as O2. The Permeameter is made up of a stainless steel membrane permeation cell that separates rates upstream (feed side) and downstream (drain side) (permeate side). To measure the pressure change with time on the permeate side, a pressure transducer is connected to the downstream side. The permeation cell exposes a 13.302 cm2 membrane area to the gas. Fig. 3 depicts the gas separation unit.

The pure gas permeation was measured using the constant-volume variable pressure method [68–70]. The raw data was used to plot the pressure increase over time. The following equation is used to calculate gas permeability:

ce:section.ce:section-title: FT-IR of P(AM-co-HEMA)/Chitosan semi-IPN hydrogel films
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectroscopy was used to study the modification that occurred after the synthesized copolymers, and the FTIR curves are shown in Fig. 4 . Synthesized hydrogel samples have characteristic peaks in their absorption spectra. The characteristic absorption band of –CH2- stretching on the backbone is related to the absorption band at 3000-2942 cm−1. The overlapping vibration of O–H and N–H stretching is responsible for the band at 3347 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of the CO and C–O groups were assigned to the absorption peaks at 1670 cm−1 and 1069 cm−1, respectively. The expected structures of the copolymer can be confirmed based on the FTIR result.

The material is developed for the packaging application, hence it is important to study the transparency. The optical transparency of the samples was measured at 570 nm. The optical transparency of the M@ZnO-0 is very good, according to the results shown in Table 3 . As the ZnO content of the film increased, so did the transparency level. ZnO NPs reduces the transparency of (P(AM-co-HEMA)/chitosan films. In terms of safety, the prepared copolymeric films will outperform traditional food packaging methods. The food that has been packed has a much longer shelf life. The transparency investigation is being carried out because the packed food in the synthesized films appears appealing and is being used for branding and pilferage.

To understand the mechanical strength of the prepared copolymeric films tensile strength and % elongation parameters have been measured. Mechanical properties for all polymeric membranes were recorded according to ASTM D 412 and are tabulated in Table 4 . Tensile strength is least for M@ZnO-0 and increases in the order M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10, whereas elongation at break is least for M@ZnO-10 and increases in the order M@ZnO-5, M@ZnO-2.5, and found highest for M@ZnO-0. This behavior could be attributed to increased membrane material is become tougher as the % loading of ZnO increases in the base polymer matrix.

The prepared films are flexible and increase intermolecular spacing due to the internal hydrogen bonding [71]. The obtained results show that increasing the ZnO content in the copolymeric membrane increases the tensile strength. This could be because the addition of ZnO NP's traps in between the polymeric chains and acts as a composite, increasing polymer film stiffness while reducing % elongation.

To understand the surface morphology of the prepared nanocomposite films SEM analysis has been carried out. The prepared films were transparent, and the films obtained were homogeneous and odorless. As the concentration of ZnO increased, the films became more flexible as expected due to internal hydrogen bonding and an increase in intermolecular spacing; thus, the films became more flexible. The internal plasticization effect of the nano-sized ZnO incorporation in between inters polymeric chain segments results in the flexibility of the membranes loaded with ZnO.

Fig. 5 shows an SEM image of the surface of M@ZnO-0 and M@ZnO-5 copolymeric nanocomposite membranes. Fig. 5 (a) depicts SEM surface images of a pristine P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymer (M@ZnO-0) taken as a control, demonstrating that the HEMA and AAm are perfectly blended with no phase separation. Fig. 5 (b) depicts M@ZnO-5 membranes. By SEM images it was observed that the ZnO NPs are impregnated in the copolymers and are found to be uniformly distributed across the base matrix.

The close-up images of the membranes with ZnO nanoparticles were shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6(a) shows the M@ZnO-5 and Fig. 6(b) represents the M@ZnO-10 membrane and in both the images we can observe the ZnO NP's very clear. The ZnO NPs are perfectly mounted in dept in the copolymeric membrane matrix. By this observation, the incorporated ZnO NPs were established in the network with the copolymer and this will give evidence for the formation of the nanocomposites.

To understand the developed nanocomposite membranes' capacity to perform antimicrobial activity this study has been carried out. Antimicrobial activity tests were performed three times on the M@ZnO-0, M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 copolymeric nanocomposite membranes. M@ZnO-0 is taken as control and for the control membrane, inhibited zone was discovered against E. Coli and S. aureus and the membrane was badly affected by these microorganisms and this is observed in Fig. 7 (a) . The no inhibition zone against E. Coli and S. aureus is observed in the case of M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 copolymeric nanocomposite membranes. Microbe colonies are consistently formed more than the plate, as well as the model's boundaries. The results of the tests show that the incorporation of ZnO in the copolymeric nanocomposite membranes is proven as a good barrier to antimicrobial activity.

Antimicrobial activity tests on M@ZnO-0 and M@ZnO-10 samples are performed. The microorganism colonies were grown uniformly across the plate, matching the border of the samples. These results show that when ZnO NPs are used in a copolymeric matrix for packaging applications, no antimicrobial effect occurred. These findings are consistent with those of antimicrobial tests against Salmonella typhimurium, Listeria monocytogenes, S. aureus, and E. coli [72,73]. The plain blend membrane i.e. M@ZnO-0 was affected by microorganisms, but the M@ZnO-10 was not affected, and Fig. 7(b) evidence that the ZnO-loaded copolymeric nanocomposite membranes were shown clear resistance to develop antimicrobial colonies in its surroundings in the same plate fruit pieces of orange and black grapes were added just to compare and were badly affected.

To understand the degradation profile of the prepared nanocomposite copolymeric membrane, a soil burial study has been carried out. For the soil burial test, P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymeric membranes were evaluated. Microorganisms feed on the copolymeric membranes, beginning with the cassava particles, causing them to lose their structural properties. The P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymeric blend membranes degrade due to the quitting of HEMA moieties, which are replaced by microbes or water molecules. As expected, the water absorbed by the blended membranes during the experiment caused swelling of the blended membranes, which aided in the biodegradation process. Chitosan is a natural polysaccharide material made up of β-(1–4) linked d-glucosamine (deacetylated branch(and N-acetyl-d-glucosamine (acetylated branch) [74]. Many bacteria and fungi in the soil environment can degrade chitosan's major constituents. Microorganisms can attack polymers and have very specific enzymes capable of hydrolyzing carbohydrate polymers into digestible small units, resulting in weakened chitosan structure and delayed chitosan strength.

The P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymeric nanocomposite membranes incorporated with ZnO NP's with higher percentages of ZnO (10 wt. %) content are expected to degrade very slowly as compared to nanocomposite membranes of M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5 nanocomposite membranes as compared to the nanocomposite membranes with higher ZnO NP's loading in the 10 and 20 days, during which the ZnO resisted to decay the membrane easily and gradual decrease has been observed over the last 20 days. The M@ZnO-0 lost its tensile strength by 15.64% for 10 days and a 30.77% loss of tensile strength was observed at the 20th-day measurement. Similarly, M@ZnO-2.5 has lost its tensile strength by 11.75% and 23.63 for 10 days and 20 days respectively. The same trend was followed for the M@ZnO-5 by a loss of tensile strength of 10.58% and 21.33 for 10 days and 20 days respectively. Amongst the observed loss in tensile strength is observed lowest by M@ZnO-10 i.e. 5.34 and 13.21 for 10 days and 20 days respectively. This observation is due to the incorporation of ZnO NPs on the base copolymeric membrane matrix. The ZnO NPs are perfectly blended and made composite with the copolymer as a result the M@ZnO-10 shows the minimum loss of tensile strength. The trend of tensile strength is M@ZnO-10 > M@ZnO-5 > M@ZnO-2.5 > M@ZnO-0. The measured results were tabulated in Table 5 .

The actual loss in tensile strength in the case of M@ZnO-0 is 38.3 MPa and 31.43 MPa for the 10 and 20-day soil burial test. For M@ZnO-2.5 it is 45.89 MPa and 39.71 MPa for 10 and 20-day soil burial tests. The decrement in tensile strength for M@ZnO-5 is 68.42 MPa and 59.28 MPa for the 10 and 20-day soil burial test. Similarly for M@ZnO-10 85.76 MPa and 78.63 MPa loss of tensile strength are observed for 10 and 20-day soil burial tests respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 8 . The decay of the copolymeric nanocomposite membrane is explained in the antimicrobial test section. By the measured values it is confirmed the membranes are found to be biodegradable.

The gas permeability test has been carried out in understanding the gas permeation property of the prepared films. The fabricated nanocomposite for M@ZnO-0, M@ZnO-2.5, M@ZnO-5, and M@ZnO-10 copolymers were tested for O2 gas permeability. Table 6 displays the permeability data for the O2 gases.

By varying the feed pressures while maintaining the varying pressure differential across the membrane surface, the prepared copolymeric nanocomposite membranes were tested for O2 gas permeability. Table 4 depicts the permeability (K). The pressure was increased from 1 to 20 kg/cm2 in the case of the M@ZnO-0 membrane and the corresponding O2 permeability is 0.0963–0.2634. The permeability at 1 kg/cm2 pressure was 0.0963 Barrer, which increased to 0.0971, 0.1192, 0.1523, 0.2322, and 0.2634 at varying feed pressure of 1, 1.5, 2, 5, 10, and 20 kg/cm2 respectively. In the case of the M@ZnO-5 and M@ZnO-10 membranes no permeability has been observed at the feed pressure of 1 and as high as 2 kg/cm2 and 0.0341, 0.0364, and 0.0364 for feed pressures at 5, 10, and 20 kg/cm2 for M@ZnO-10. Copolymeric nanocomposite membranes have a negligible oxygen permeability rate even at higher feed pressures. As a result of this finding, we can conclude that the synthesized copolymeric nanocomposite membranes are a good candidate for the packaging of fruits and vegetables to increase their shelf life. The purpose of this study is to understand the permeability of oxygen through the membrane so that flushing with oxygen in packed fruits and vegetable will remains fresh for a longer time and improvement in the shelf life of the items.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3.8 Comparison of gas Permeability with commercial sample and literature
ce:section.ce:para: The data obtained for the fabricated nanocomposite membranes are compared in terms of O2 permeability with commercial sample i.e. low-density polyethylene (LDPE) which is generally used as packing material. Also, the obtained results are compared with those reported in the literature. For the comparison purpose, the values taken are measured feed pressure at 1 kg/cm2. The obtained data were tabulated in Table 7 . M@ZnO-0 showed the 0.0963 Barrer and M@ZnO-10 performed no permeation towards O2. Plain PVA showed 0.8740 Barrer, PVA-g-St-g-PMA showed 1.8860 Barrer, Hydroxyethyl Cellulose-Hollow Silica membrane showed 2490 Barrer and PVA-g-AAm-40 performed with 0.0072 Barrer. The commercial sample LDPE was 0.1321 Barrer O2 permeability. As compared to all the readings the fabricated nanocomposite membranes showed better results.

The P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan semi-IPN copolymeric nanocomposite membrane embedded with ZnO NP's was investigated for food packaging application in the current research work. In an attempt to improve the new generation of food packaging material, which should be good tensile strength, antimicrobial and biodegradability, and gas permeation properties have been measured. The oxygen barrier property of the membranes was tested and found to be oxygen barrier and no permeation has been recorded up to 1.5 kg/cm2 feed pressure. The biodegradability of the various copolymeric nanocomposite membranes was tested by keeping plain P(AAm-co-HEMA)/chitosan membrane as a placebo. The synthesized nanocomposite membranes were proven as biodegradable. The antimicrobial test demonstrates that the ZnO NP-loaded membranes are suitable materials for antimicrobial fruits and vegetable packaging materials. The fabricated copolymeric nanocomposite membranes exhibit flexibility, oxygen barrier property, and biodegradability, they have proven as a substrate or as new generation packaging materials in vegetable packaging as well as processed food. These membranes are expected to be used in antimicrobial food packaging as well as biomedical applications.
